results.title: Top Three Video Subtitling Mistakes That Beginners Make | Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey
results.content:
Logo for Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey

Search

# Top Three Video Subtitling Mistakes That Beginners Make

| by Max Troyer

News Stories

Example of bad subtitling, created for purposes of illustration 

This subtitle breaks all three rules.

Have you ever watched a foreign film and spotted an error in the subtitles?

You’re not alone. Online video production is skyrocketing, and it’s driving demand for people who can create high-quality subtitles in multiple languages. However, right now, it is easy to find examples of subtitles gone wrong (like these). As a professor in Translation and Localization Management at the Middlebury Institute, I’ve taught hundreds of students how to create quality subtitles. [...] ## 2. The subtitles aren’t synced with scene changes.

The human eye is distracted when camera angles change and there are cuts between scenes. That’s why it’s recommended that there be a two-frame gap between scenes to give the viewer’s eyes a chance to detect that a new subtitle is on screen. This is also why subtitles can’t start or end too close to a scene change, as it would be very distracting to the view. Timing subtitles to synchronize with the visuals and fade into the background takes some creativity and practice .

## 3. The subtitles have line breaks that split key information across too many lines or frames. [...] These are the top three mistakes that beginners make:

## 1. The subtitle reading speed is too fast.

Good subtitles are all about comprehension, and people can only process information so quickly, especially when they are also trying to watch the visuals.

How fast is the ideal reading speed for subtitles? Adults can handle a reading rate of 20 characters per second (CPS) in English, according to research. Young people read more slowly, and foreign languages have different targets for characters per second. This is also why subtitles often don’t correspond to the dialog—they’re simplified to comply with reading speed limits.

## 2. The subtitles aren’t synced with scene changes.

results.title: Subtitling best practices: Your go-to guide
results.content:
#### 4) Get timing and synchronization right

Even the best-structured subtitles fail if they’re not properly timed. Each subtitle should appear in sync with the dialogue and stay visible long enough for viewers to read comfortably – usually between one and eight seconds. Netflix, for example, recommends a minimum duration of five-sixths of a second and a maximum of seven seconds per subtitle.

When subtitles show up too early or too late, it becomes harder to follow what’s happening on screen. Viewers start focusing on the text instead of the action and can easily miss key visual cues or moments. Poor timing also makes dialogue feel out of sync, which can make a polished video seem off. [...] Use them only when needed. Overusing speaker IDs can clutter the screen or distract from the visuals, especially when the speaker is already visible. The goal is to support comprehension, not add visual noise.

Tip: Define a clear formatting style for speaker IDs in your subtitling guide. Whether you use brackets, colons or capitalization, keep it consistent so viewers always know what to expect.

#### 6) Double-check your subtitles

No matter how advanced your tools or workflows are, nothing replaces a final human review. Subtitles are only as good as the eyes that check them. A quick proofread can catch issues that slip through automated QA, from typos and timing errors to mistranslations and awkward phrasing. [...] Subtitles should be easy to read in any setting – whether your video shows a bright snowfield, a forest trail at dusk or a studio interview. For most content, simple white sans-serif text with a thin outline or a semi-transparent background works best. It stands out without distracting from the visuals.

Creative effects or trendy styles often look good in isolation but make reading harder once the footage starts moving. Tiny fonts, flashy colours or fast single-word captions – common in subtitling for YouTube, TikTok or social media videos – might catch attention but quickly tire viewers out or obscure meaning.

results.title: Why is Closed Captioning Not Working? Fix Subtitle Errors Fast (2026)
results.content:
### How do I tell if a video even supports subtitles

Look for the "CC" logo on the video's description page. If that icon isn't there, the video likely doesn't have a subtitle track available, and no amount of settings-toggling will make them appear.

Categories

## Categories [...] Try exporting a small segment of the video first or checking your app version. Often, a quick update to the latest version of CapCut resolves bugs with the captioning tool.

## What should I know about captions in other places

Captions aren't always just text on a screen; sometimes the format changes based on where you are.

### What does "Closed Caption" actually mean at a movie theater

In a theater, "Closed" means the captions are only visible to the person who needs them. You are usually given a small device that fits in your cup holder or a pair of glasses that project the text.
